document.title: Unknown
document.abstract: Often in life, we are faced with the problem of finding the best way to do something. For example, a farmer wants to choose a mix of crops that is likely to produce the largest profit. A doctor wishes to select the smallest dosage of a drug that will cure a certain disease. Often such a problem can be formulated so that it involves maximizing or minimizing a function over a specified set. If so, the methods of calculus provide a powerful tool for solving the problem. DEFINITION 7.1. Let S, the domain of f, contain the point c. We say that: 1) f(c) is the maximum value of f on S if 𝑓 𝑐 ≥ 𝑓(𝑥) for all x in S 2) f(c) is the minimum value of f on S if 𝑓 𝑐 ≤ 𝑓(𝑥) for all x in S 3) F(c) is an extreme value of f on S if it is either the maximum value or minimum value
document.full_text:
If f is continuous on a closed interval [a, b], then f attains both a maximum value and a minimum value there.
How to find the points, where the function attains these extreme values?
Let f be defined on an interval I containing the point c. If f(c) is an extreme value, then c must be a critical point; that is, either c is:
1) an end point of I; 2) a stationary point of f; that is, a point where f'(c) = 0; or 3) a singular point of f; that is, a point where f'(c) does not exist.
(necessary condition for the existance of the extreme value) Let f be defined on an interval I. If a differentiable function attains its extreme value at the point c anc c is not an end point, then 𝑓 ′ 𝑐 = 0.
Should be taken into account that, the necessary condition does not guarantee that at a point where the derivative is 0 the extreme value will be attained.
Therefore, for finding the maximum and minimum value of a continuous function f on a closed interval I: 1) Find the critical points of f on I, 2) Evaluate f at each of these critical points. The largest of these values is the maximum value; the smales is the minimum value.
Please, find extremal values of the function 𝑓 𝑥 = 2𝑥 3 -24𝑥 + 1 on the interval [-3;1].
Find the derivative 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) = 6𝑥 2 -24, equal it to 0 and find two stationary points -2 and 2, but 2 ∉ -3; 1 . Thus, the critical points are -3, -2 and 1. Find values of the function at these points.
We can conclude that the maximum value of the function 𝑓 𝑥 = 2𝑥 3 -24𝑥 + 1 on the interval [-3;1] is 𝑓 -2 = 33, but the minimum value is 𝑓 1 = -21. Answer:
Please, find extremal values of the function 𝑓 𝑥 = 𝑥 2 ;9 𝑥 2 ;2 on the interval [3;5].
Let f be defined on an interval I (open, closed, or neither). We say that: 1) f is increasing on I if, for every pair of numbers 𝑥 1 and 𝑥 2 in I 𝑥 1 < 𝑥 2 ⇒ 𝑓 𝑥 1 < 𝑓(𝑥 2 ); 2) f is decreasing on I if, for every pair of numbers 𝑥 1 and 𝑥 2 in I 𝑥 1 < 𝑥 2 ⇒ 𝑓 𝑥 1 > 𝑓(𝑥 2 ); 3) f is strictly monotonic on I if it is either increasing on I or decreasing on I. Let f be continuous on an interval I and differentiable at every interior point. 1) If 𝑓 ′ 𝑥 > 0 for all x interior to I, then f is increasing on I.
2) If 𝑓 ′ 𝑥 < 0 for all x interior to I, then f is decreasing on I.
□ Let's suppose that ∀𝑥 ∈ 𝑎; 𝑏 𝑓 ′ 𝑥 > 0 and le'ts choose two arbitrary points 𝑥 1 , 𝑥 2 ∈ (𝑎; 𝑏) such that 𝑥 1 < 𝑥 2 .
In accordance with mean value theorem for derivatives ∃𝑐 ∈ 𝑥 1 , 𝑥 2 𝑓 ′ 𝑐 = 𝑓 𝑥 2 ;𝑓(𝑥 1 ) 𝑥 2 ;𝑥 1 .
As for every 𝑥 ∈ 𝑎; 𝑏 𝑓 ′ 𝑥 > 0, then also 𝑓 ′ 𝑐 > 0. What means that 𝑓 𝑥 2 ;𝑓(𝑥 1 ) 𝑥 2 ;𝑥 1 > 0.
As 𝑥 1 < 𝑥 2 , that is, 𝑥 2 -𝑥 1 > 0, then we have to conclude that 𝑓(𝑥 1 ) < 𝑓(𝑥 2 ), which means that function f(x) is increasing on interval (a; b). Similarly can prove the 2).■
Please, detect the increasing and decreasing intervals of the function 𝑓 𝑥 = 4𝑥:5 1;𝑥 2 . Let's find the derivative Let's find the critical points: 1) Stationary points (s where f'(c) = 0)by finding roots of the equation [, decreasing terval ]-1;0[ 14. The area of the cardboard page is 8 cm x 5 cm. By cutting out identical squares from the four corners and turning up the sides should be made a box with the maximum volume. Please find the length of the square side that shoud be cut out from the corners. Answer: 1 cm Reading: D. Varberg, E. J. Purcell, S. E. Rigdon Calculus. 9th Edition, Prentice-Hall, Inc.m, 2014, p. 151-156, 167 -168, 185 -188. Chapter 3. Sections 3.1., 3.2., 3.4., 3.6.
